turns.text: Hello Alyssa, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thanks for reaching out. Honestly, it’s a bit strange to put all this into words. Usually, I just keep it bottled up so I don’t scare the kids or put more on my husband’s plate. 

I’m in my second trimester, and I’m twenty-nine, but I feel like I’ve aged a decade in the last few months. This is my fifth, and you’d think the nerves would have settled by now, right? But this time is different. I’m marked "high risk," and every time I walk into that clinic, it feels like I’m just a checklist of potential disasters. My jaw has been throbbing for weeks from a wisdom tooth I can’t afford to fix, and the midwife keeps talking about my iron levels and diet like I’m choosing to be anemic. She doesn’t see the grocery store flyer in my hand, trying to figure out how to feed four growing kids *and* buy the lean meat she’s recommending. 

I’m terrified of the blood pressure cuff. Every time it tightens, I hold my breath, just praying the numbers haven't spiked again. The thought of being admitted for preeclampsia—being away from my family—keeps me up at night. I want to trust the doctors, I really do, but sometimes I feel like they don’t see me, just the chart. It's just a lot to carry, you know?

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

When I first saw that positive test, my heart just sank a little. I knew right away this one would be different because of everything I’ve been through before. I guess I was hoping for... I don’t know, a bit more of a partnership, I suppose? I expected someone to sit down with me, look at the whole picture—not just the blood pressure or the weight—and say, "Okay, Alyssa, we know money is tight and you’ve got four kids at home, let’s figure out a way to get you through this safely."

But what it’s actually been like is just clinical. It’s hurry up and wait in the lobby, then quick-fire questions in a sterile room that smells like antiseptic. They run the tests, they frown at the numbers, and then they give me these pamphlets about nutrition that feel like they were written for someone who doesn’t have to worry about a grocery budget. 

It feels like they’re reading off a script, checking boxes to keep themselves protected legally, but they aren't looking at me. They don't see the woman who’s already exhausted from working and chasing four kids. I leave those appointments feeling smaller than when I walked in, just another high-risk chart on their pile. It’s cold, honestly. Very cold.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I guess I expected it to be a conversation. You know, like, "Here’s what the tests showed, here’s why it matters for the baby, and here’s how we’re going to handle it together." I thought I’d feel like a member of the team, someone they’d actually explain things to so I wouldn't have to spend all night Googling stuff that just makes my anxiety worse.

The reality? It’s been… abrupt. I get a call or a quick note in the patient portal, and it’s usually just numbers and medical jargon. When I do get a face-to-face, it’s like they’re waiting for me to panic. If my numbers aren't "perfect"—which, let's be honest, they rarely are—the focus is immediately on the danger. It’s all "If this continues, we’ll have to do this, or we might have to induce then." It’s never, "You’re doing a great job, let’s manage this." 

It makes me feel like I’m failing before I’ve even started. They talk *at* me about the risks, but they don’t explain how I’m supposed to change my reality to meet their expectations. I leave the office feeling like I’m sitting on a ticking time bomb, and honestly, that fear is harder to carry than the pregnancy itself.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

There is a massive gap, and it feels like a canyon. I get these snippets of information—"your protein is up," "your iron is low," "watch your stress"—but it’s like being handed a piece of a puzzle without seeing the picture on the box. They give me these clinical warnings, but they never bridge the gap between "you need better nutrition" and the reality of my kitchen at home. 

I’m the one left at 2:00 AM, jaw aching, scrolling through forums on my phone, trying to decode what the nurse said in five seconds flat. I don't need another pamphlet; I need someone to listen to why I'm struggling to get those specific foods. I need to know *why* my blood pressure is doing what it's doing in a way that doesn't just make me feel like I’m a broken machine. 

When you’re high-risk, information is supposed to be power, right? But here, it just feels like fear. They give me enough info to be scared, but never enough to feel empowered or capable. It leaves me feeling paralyzed, like I’m waiting for the next shoe to drop, and nobody is there to tell me how to stop it. It’s lonely, really.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I think the biggest dream I had was that I’d have one person—one face I could recognize—who would actually remember my name without looking at my chart. I wanted someone who knew that this is my fifth time around, who remembered the struggles I had before, and who could see that my stress isn't just "pregnancy anxiety," it's life anxiety. I expected a midwife who would ask, "How are the kids?" and mean it, not just as a way to get me to stop talking about my actual worries.

The reality? It’s a revolving door. Every appointment feels like a fresh start with a stranger. One time I see a midwife, the next it’s a nurse practitioner, and then maybe a doctor I’ve never spoken to before. I have to re-explain my whole history every single time. "Yes, this is my fifth. Yes, I had issues with preeclampsia before. Yes, I'm working." It’s exhausting to retell your life story to someone who looks like they’re just trying to get through the next ten minutes so they can get to their next patient. 

There’s zero continuity. I’m not a person to them; I’m a file that gets passed around. Nobody knows the *whole* Alyssa, just the high-risk parts. It’s impossible to feel safe when you’re constantly introducing yourself to the people who are supposed to be responsible for your life and your baby’s.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

It’s just… it’s exhausting. Practically speaking, it’s a waste of time. I spend the first ten minutes of every single visit playing catch-up, explaining my medical history while they frantically flip through my file. I can see them glancing at the clock, clearly frustrated that they have to get up to speed on "me" instead of just moving on to the next task. By the time they actually listen to the baby or check my vitals, the appointment is practically over.

Emotionally, it’s isolating. How am I supposed to talk about the real stuff—the paralyzing fear of the delivery, or the fact that I’m struggling to afford groceries—when I’m talking to a stranger? I can't be vulnerable with someone who doesn't even know my name without checking the screen. 

When you don’t have that one consistent person to advocate for you, you feel like you’re drifting. I don't have anyone to say, "Hey, I remember you mentioned this last time, how did it go?" It makes the whole process feel so transactional. I’m just a body in a gown to them. It keeps my walls up, and I think that’s why I leave every appointment feeling more anxious than when I walked in. I’m carrying all this alone.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected them to treat my body like a partner in this, you know? Like, "Your body is working hard, let’s see what it needs." I thought there would be some empathy for the swelling, or maybe even a comment on how much energy it takes to grow a fifth baby. I wanted to feel respected, like my body was something to be cared for, not just a problem to be fixed.

Instead, the interactions feel like a critique. When they see the swelling in my ankles, it isn’t "I’m sorry you’re uncomfortable," it’s a clinical note followed by a warning about what might happen if it gets worse. It’s all about the numbers on the scale, the blood pressure cuff, the urine samples. Everything feels like a test I’m failing. They touch my stomach to measure the baby, but they never really *look* at me. I feel like they’re looking at a car engine that’s misfiring, searching for the broken part. 

It makes me ashamed of my own skin. I look at my body in the mirror and I see the fear and the fatigue, and then I go in there and they turn it into a list of "risks." It feels incredibly invasive and cold. It’s like they’re watching for my body to betray me, and honestly, that’s exactly what I’m scared of, too.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was this one appointment a few weeks ago that really stuck with me. A new doctor, someone I hadn't met, was doing the exam. She was looking at my chart and just started firing off questions about my weight gain and the swelling in my legs without even making eye contact. 

She told me, very bluntly, that I needed to be "more disciplined" with my diet if I wanted to avoid ending up on bed rest or in the hospital with preeclampsia. She didn't ask what I was eating; she just assumed I wasn't trying. She didn't acknowledge how hard it is to work full-time while you're this far along, or that sometimes, yeah, I’m just too exhausted to cook from scratch. 

I felt so small. It felt like she was blaming me for the potential complications, like the swelling and the high blood pressure were just a result of me being lazy or careless. I wanted to cry right there on the table, but I just nodded and apologized. I felt like a child being scolded. I wasn't prepared for her to treat my physical struggle as a moral failing. I left the clinic and sat in my car for twenty minutes just trying to stop my hands from shaking. I just wanted help, not a lecture on my "discipline."

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I think I expected it to be a manageable, structured process. I thought, "Okay, if I’m high-risk, I’ll have a team who is going to hold my hand through the hurdles." I figured there would be clear instructions, maybe a nutritionist to help me navigate the budget-friendly healthy eating, or even just a nurse I could call when I felt lightheaded so I wouldn't have to guess if it was an emergency. I thought the medical system would be a safety net.

The reality? Managing it is like trying to navigate a maze in the dark. There’s no team, just me and my "what-ifs." I’m the one trying to figure out which foods are both "approved" and actually affordable, while balancing the stress of work and the kids. When they mention the sugar tests or the preeclampsia monitoring, they hand me a stack of papers and send me home. It’s all on me to execute this perfect plan, and when I can’t—because I’m tired, or the money isn't there, or the baby is just making everything harder—I’m the one who feels the weight of the "non-compliance." 

It’s not a partnership; it’s a burden. They tell me the *what*—like "keep your blood pressure down"—but they don't give me the *how* that fits into a life like mine. It just makes the whole condition feel like my personal failure.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected that if I was labeled "high risk," there would be some kind of direct line. You know, like, "If you feel weird or your head is pounding, call us, we’re right here." I figured that being in a higher-risk category meant the system would be more vigilant, maybe even more accessible, because they know how fast things can go wrong.

But the reality is a nightmare of automated phone trees and waiting on hold for twenty minutes just to talk to a receptionist who tells me they’ll "leave a message for the nurse." By the time I get a call back—if I get one at all—I’ve either panicked myself into a full-blown headache or just decided to ignore it because I don't want to be "that patient" who's always complaining. 

It feels like the system is designed to keep me at arm's length. Even when I’m terrified that something is off, I’m just another number in their queue. It makes me feel like I’m completely on my own. I end up second-guessing every symptom—*Is this just normal pregnancy aches, or is my blood pressure spiking again? Should I go to the ER or will they just send me home with a bill I can't pay?* It’s exhausting to have to act like my own doctor when I’m the one who’s supposed to be under their care.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

Oh, definitely. I do it all the time. Just last week, I had this really intense, sharp pain in my side, and my head was pounding—the kind that makes you see spots. My first instinct was to call the office, but then I thought about the hoops I’d have to jump through. I imagined sitting on hold, then explaining myself to someone who doesn't know me, then waiting for a callback that might come hours later. And honestly? I was afraid that if I *did* go in, they’d just look at my blood pressure, see it’s a bit high, and give me that same lecture about how I need to "relax" and "eat better," like it’s as simple as flipping a switch.

It’s humiliating to keep calling and feeling like you’re a nuisance. I don’t want to be the "problem patient" who cries wolf. So, I laid down in the dark instead. I drank a glass of water, stared at the ceiling, and just hoped it would pass. I sat there in the silence, terrified that if something was actually wrong, I was making the wrong choice—but I was more scared of the judgment and the stress of navigating their system. It’s a terrible feeling, lying there, wondering if you’re risking your baby’s life because you’re too tired to fight the system.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I think I expected things to be a bit more like how my own mother described it—a village, you know? I really hoped that with my fifth, the people around me would step in to take some of the weight off. Not just babysitting, but maybe bringing a meal that actually fit the doctor's "dietary requirements" or just sitting with me for an hour so I didn't feel so incredibly trapped by my own fear.

But the reality is that everyone is fighting their own battles. My husband is working overtime just to keep us afloat, so he’s exhausted too—he doesn't have the energy to hold space for my anxiety when he’s worried about the rent. My friends are busy with their own families, and I don't want to be the "always sick, always complaining" pregnant woman. I feel guilty even asking for help.

So, I’ve just learned to keep it quiet. I put on a brave face, I handle the kids, I manage the house, and I keep the fear locked behind my teeth. When you’re low-income and high-risk, people expect you to just be "resilient." They don't see the silent cracks. I don't want to be a burden, so I just handle it alone, even when the loneliness is the heaviest part of the day.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I think what I expected most was a bit of grace—you know, someone to just say, "I see how hard you’re working to keep this baby healthy on what you have." I really wanted someone to take the groceries off my plate just once, or maybe watch the kids while I actually rested instead of doing "chores" that I call resting. But that support just never materialized. People see me, they see the four kids, and they assume I’ve got it all handled, that I’m some kind of super-mom who doesn’t need anything. It’s isolating when you’re drowning, but everyone around you assumes you’re just swimming.

What I didn't expect, though, was the judgment that sometimes comes from family. People hear "high-risk pregnancy," and instead of offering a hand, they offer unsolicited advice or even tell me, "Maybe you shouldn't have gotten pregnant again." It feels like a punch in the gut. I already feel guilty about the money and the stress, and when I get that kind of feedback, it makes me retreat even further into my own head. 

I’ve had a neighbor who occasionally drops off a bag of apples or some fresh greens when she hits a sale—that was a surprise, honestly. She doesn’t ask questions, she just leaves them on the porch. That meant more than any pamphlet a doctor ever handed me. But mostly? I’m just trying to hold up the world, and I’m terrified of what happens if I drop it.

It’s just… the longer this goes on, the more I feel like I’m in a race I’m already losing. My baby is growing, but I’m just shrinking. Does that make sense? Every time I go to the doctor and they talk about the "risks" and the "outcomes," I just want to ask them, "Can you help me get through today?" 

I’m sitting here looking at the kitchen table, and I’m thinking about that wisdom tooth again. It’s a small thing compared to preeclampsia, but it’s a constant, pulsing reminder that I can’t even take care of myself. If I can't afford to take the pain away, how am I supposed to stay calm enough to keep my blood pressure down for the baby? It feels like the system expects me to be this perfect, stress-free, well-nourished woman, but I’m just Alyssa. I’m tired, I’m worried about the money, and I’m scared to death of what might happen during delivery.

I just wish one of them would look me in the eye and say, "I know this is hard, and I’m here to help you get through it," instead of pointing out everything that’s going wrong. I just want to feel like I’m enough. I want my baby to be safe, and I want to be around to see them grow up with the rest of my kids. That’s all. But right now, the "what-ifs" are so loud, they’re the only thing I can hear.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

It feels like that’s the story of my life lately. My voice feels so small compared to theirs. When I’m at the clinic, the doctor’s opinion isn't just an opinion—it's like a law they're handing down. I remember one appointment where I tried to explain that I was feeling dizzy, not just from my iron, but because I’d been working extra shifts to pay for the last batch of prescriptions. I wanted to talk about finding a way to balance that.

Instead, the doctor just cut me off. She told me, "Your priority needs to be the pregnancy, not the work." Like I didn't already know that! Like I haven't been lying awake at night figuring out which bill to skip so I can eat better. It wasn't advice; it was a reprimand. She acted like my life circumstances were choices I was making against the baby, rather than the reality I’m trapped in. 

And at home? It’s different, but still hard. My husband, he loves me, but when he sees me stressed or crying, he just says, "Just stop worrying, it’s not good for the baby." I know he means well, but hearing that just makes me feel more isolated. It feels like everyone—my doctors, my family—wants me to be this calm, perfect vessel, and if I’m not, it’s because I’m failing. My feelings, my fatigue, my fear—it’s all treated like something I should just suppress. I’m carrying all this worry, but I’m not allowed to even *talk* about it because it’s "bad for the baby." It makes me feel like I’m disappearing.

You know, the hardest part is that I’m trying so hard to be perfect for this baby, but every day feels like I’m walking a tightrope with no net. I look at those grocery flyers, and I see "fresh salmon" or "organic spinach," and I just feel this sharp, physical sting of guilt. How am I supposed to nourish a baby when I’m barely keeping the lights on? It’s not just about the food—it’s the shame. It’s like the medical system looks at my pantry and thinks I’m failing the baby, but they don’t understand that I’m choosing between my own health and the kids' school supplies.

When I’m in that clinic, I just want someone to stop looking at their clipboard and look at *me*. I want someone to ask, "Alyssa, what can we do to help *you* get to next week?" instead of telling me what I'm doing wrong. The "what-ifs" are so loud—*What if my pressure spikes tonight? What if I end up in the hospital and there’s no one to watch the kids? What if I’m not strong enough to do this again?* 

Sometimes, I sit here in the kitchen after the kids are in bed, and I just feel this deep, cold ache. I don't want to be a chart, or a high-risk case, or a statistic. I just want to be a mom who can finally breathe again. I just want us both to be okay. It’s all I’ve got, really. Just this hope that somehow, it’ll be enough.
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

You know, I don’t think my husband is doing it on purpose—he’s just scared, too, and he thinks that by being "positive" and telling me to just "keep it together," he’s protecting me. But it feels like my own body, my own pain, and my own worries are being pushed aside because they’re uncomfortable for him to hear. 

When it comes to the decisions—like whether I should keep working those extra shifts or just quit and risk the bills—there’s this pressure to prioritize the baby’s safety above everything else, which I absolutely want. But he, like the doctors, doesn't always see the cost. He says, "Alyssa, you need to be home, you need to rest, your health comes first." And he's right! But he doesn't have to look at the bank account. He doesn't have to look at the kids' shoes that are wearing thin. 

It feels like a trap. I feel the pressure to be this perfect, resting, healthy incubator, but the reality of my life—the rent, the kids, the groceries—doesn't let me be that. So I feel like I'm failing the "ideal pregnancy" that he and the doctors want. I end up compromising my own sanity, working while I’m exhausted, just to keep the peace and keep us fed. It feels like my own survival is getting lost in everyone’s expectations. I’m just trying to hold it all together, and sometimes I don't know who I’m doing it for anymore—the baby, my husband, or just to keep everyone from worrying.

It’s exhausting, honestly, feeling like my life is being pulled in two directions by people who don't actually live in my house. My doctor says "relax and eat better," and my husband says "just take it easy," but neither of them ever asks how I’m supposed to pay for the extra food or how I’m supposed to "relax" when there’s a mountain of laundry and bills that don't stop growing just because I’m pregnant. 

I’m sitting here right now, looking at my daughter’s sneakers by the door, and they’re so worn out. I know I need to buy her a new pair soon, and that’s another twenty or thirty dollars that isn't going toward the iron-rich food the midwife keeps stressing about. It feels like every single decision I make is a trade-off. If I take care of the baby the way the medical books say I should, someone else in my family goes without. If I take care of my family, I’m "failing" the medical standards for a high-risk pregnancy. 

I’m so scared that one day, my body is just going to give out from the stress, and then what? Who picks up the pieces? I want this baby so much, and I love my kids with everything I have, but I feel like I’m disappearing into all of this. I just wish someone would say, "I know you're doing your best, and that is enough." Just once. That would mean more to me than any test result.

Sometimes I wonder if the medical system actually understands what "high risk" means to someone like me. To them, it’s a clinical label—a reason for extra scans, more frequent blood tests, and stricter protocols. But for me? It’s a constant, suffocating weight. It’s like living under a storm cloud that never breaks. Every appointment I have, that label just gets heavier. 

I’m so tired of the "what-ifs." I walk into the clinic feeling like a puzzle that’s missing half its pieces, and I feel like the providers are looking at the empty spots and blaming me. Why aren't my iron levels higher? Why is my pressure creeping up? I try so hard to follow their directions, but when the choice is between a healthy snack and making sure my kids have what they need for school, I feel like I'm losing the only thing I have control over: my own mothering.

I just want this to be over. I want to hold my baby, I want this pregnancy fear to go away, and I want to stop feeling like I’m constantly failing. I’m just trying to survive until the finish line, but it feels like the path is being made harder by the very people who are supposed to be helping me walk it. Is that just how it is, or am I asking for too much?
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

When I’m feeling really scared or just overwhelmed by the physical symptoms, the first place I go isn’t the clinic—it’s the internet. I’ll go on those mom forums or look at social media groups where other women talk about their pregnancies. I feel like I can be more honest there. Nobody is checking my blood pressure or judging the groceries I bought or telling me to "just relax." I can say, "I’m terrified of preeclampsia," and someone else will say, "Me too, I’ve been there." It’s the only place where I don’t feel like I’m the only person in the world going through this.

After that, I turn to my sister. She’s had a couple of kids, and she’s the one who gets it. When I’m crying over a bill or worried about my heart racing, she doesn’t look at a chart; she just listens. She doesn’t lecture me on nutrition or weight gain—she just tells me she understands. She knows we’re both trying to do the best we can with what we’ve got. 

If I called the doctor’s office with half the stuff I tell my sister, I’d be labeled "high anxiety" or "non-compliant." With them, I have to be a performance. With my sister, I can just be me, and that's the only thing that actually keeps me going when it feels like I’m breaking.

It’s really about having someone who speaks *my* language, you know? When I’m with my sister or reading in those groups, there isn't that clinical distance—that feeling like I’m a specimen they’re waiting to watch crash. They don't look at my swelling or my blood pressure numbers as a score I’m losing. They look at *me*. They ask, "Are you doing okay today, Alyssa?" and they actually wait for an answer.

I’ve had moments where I’ve sat in the clinic waiting room, listening to the nurses talk in the hallway about "the preeclampsia patient" in room four, and I realized they were talking about me. I felt like I wanted to just stand up and walk out. It’s so dehumanizing when you’re treated as a problem to be managed rather than a person trying to survive. 

I’m sitting here, and I look at the calendar, counting down the weeks until the due date, and it feels like I’m counting down the days until I can finally stop being "high risk." I just want to be me again, without the weight of the "what-ifs" looming over my shoulder. I’m doing my best, and honestly, some days that’s all I have to give. I just hope it’s enough to get us both to the finish line in one piece.

You know, I find myself standing in the kitchen, just looking at my four kids—seeing their smiles, hearing them laugh—and then the pain in my jaw hits, or I feel that lightheadedness from the anemia, and it all comes crashing back. I feel this massive, heavy pressure to stay upright for them. I want to show them that being a mother means being strong, but I feel so fragile right now.

I keep thinking about the day this is all over. I just want to walk into a room where no one is looking at a chart, where no one is measuring my belly or talking about "clinical risks." I just want to be Alyssa again—not "the patient with the high-risk history." I want to be the mom who isn't always looking over her shoulder for the next symptom or the next scary lab report. 

If I could change one thing, it wouldn’t be the medicine or the extra appointments. It would just be being treated like a human being instead of a case file. I just want someone to look at me, truly look at me, and validate how much I’m carrying. I think if I felt a little more human, a little less like a "system" problem, I’d be able to breathe a whole lot easier. I just want to know that I’m going to make it, and that my baby will be safe, and that I won't have to carry this much weight all by myself. I hope, when it's all said and done, that I’ll still recognize myself.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

It was right at my last scan, the one that’s supposed to be the "big one" for monitoring the baby’s growth because of the preeclampsia risk. I had been stressing about it for days, barely sleeping, just hoping for a bit of good news to cling to. The technician was doing the scan, and she wasn't saying a word—just moving the probe around and frowning at the screen. I asked, "Everything okay?" and she just muttered, "The doctor will talk to you." 

When the doctor finally came in, he didn't even look at me. He just grabbed the report, scribbled something on a notepad, and said, "Growth is trailing, we need to talk about induction if it doesn't pick up." He didn't explain what "trailing" meant, how much of a gap there was, or if there were things *I* could do to help. He just dropped that bomb and walked out, leaving me there with the jelly still on my stomach, completely terrified. 

I felt like my heart had been ripped out. He didn't explain the urgency, he didn't give me any space to ask questions, and he didn't even acknowledge the absolute panic I was feeling. It made me realize that to him, the baby is just a measurement, a set of percentiles on a graph. He didn't care that he just changed the entire direction of my life in five seconds. That was the moment I realized I couldn't rely on them for comfort or clarity. I felt so completely alone in that room. I haven't been able to look at the ultrasound photos since; they just remind me of how cold it felt.

It makes me wonder, if they don’t care enough to hold my hand through the hard news, how can I trust them to hold my baby? It’s like they’re waiting for something to go wrong so they can jump into action, but they aren't helping me stay upright while I’m in the thick of it.

I keep coming back to that moment in the clinic—the way he just walked out. He didn’t stop to see if I was shaking, or if I’d even understood what "induction" would mean for my life, my work, or my kids. It’s like they have this map for a pregnancy, and mine is constantly veering off into the woods, but they don't have a compass or a map for *that* version of the road. They just keep pointing at the original map and telling me I’m lost.

I’m so tired of feeling like I’m navigating a minefield, and everyone is standing on the sidelines watching to see if I trigger a blast. Sometimes I think about just canceling the next appointment. If it’s only going to be more bad news or more cold lectures, why keep going back to be hurt? But then I remember the baby, and the dread just settles back into my chest, heavier than before. It’s a vicious cycle, and I don't see a way out. I’m just trying to keep my head above water, and I’m terrified that I’m running out of air.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

Honestly, every single appointment feels like that. My husband is working so hard, pulling double shifts at the warehouse just to make sure we don't fall behind on rent. I know he’s out there trying to build a future for us, but when I’m sitting in that exam room alone, listening to the doctor list off all the ways this pregnancy is "high risk," I feel that gap so deeply.

There was one morning where they had to discuss the possibility of early induction because of my blood pressure. I was sitting on that paper-covered table, shivering, while the doctor went over the risks to the baby and the possibility of me needing an extended hospital stay. I just kept looking at the empty chair beside me. I wanted him to be there to hold my hand, to hear the doctor’s words for himself so he’d understand the fear, and to help me ask the questions I was too overwhelmed to think of. 

When I’m alone, I feel like I’m just a vessel for medical concerns. When he’s with me, I feel like a person again—his wife, the mother of his kids. Without him there to anchor me, I just float off into that dark, cold space where all the "what-ifs" live. I don’t blame him for having to work, I really don't, but I hate that the system is set up in a way that forces me to face these heavy, life-altering conversations by myself. It’s a lonely kind of motherhood, honestly, having to carry both the physical weight of the baby and the emotional weight of the risk all on my own.

You know, sometimes I look at the other women in the waiting room—the ones who have their partners right there next to them, holding their hands, asking the questions for them—and I feel this sharp, stinging envy. Not because I want their lives, but because I just want that safety net. 

When you’re high-risk, everything feels so precarious. If I have to go into the hospital early, who’s going to be there for the kids? Who’s going to make sure the rent is paid if I’m not bringing home my paycheck? My husband is out there trying to keep us from sinking, but when he’s gone and I’m sitting there in that sterile room, it feels like I’m in a fight I’m meant to lose. 

I’m so tired of being the strong one for everyone else, the one who hides the fear, who manages the appointments, who smiles at the kids so they don't see their mom is terrified. I just want to be able to lean on someone for once. I want someone to look at me and see that I’m not just a "high-risk patient," but a mother who is doing everything in her power to bring a new life into this world, even when the world feels like it’s stacked against her. Is that really too much to ask?
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

Every single time I leave that clinic, I head straight to the internet. It’s like a reflex now. My doctor gives me these vague, scary warnings about "complications" or "growth issues," but she never explains what my life actually looks like if those things happen. I’m not just looking for a definition of preeclampsia—I’ve read the pamphlets—I’m looking for the *reality* of it. 

I search for things like: "How do you manage bed rest with four kids at home?" or "What does a high-risk pregnancy mean for my paycheck?" or "Can I really prevent this, or is my body just failing?" I’m looking for stories from other women who are also living paycheck-to-paycheck, who aren't just sitting in a quiet house waiting for their "next trimester symptom" to pop up. 

I feel like the medical professionals operate in this vacuum where I don’t have a real life, a real job, or real stresses outside of their office. They don't give me the practical answers I need to survive the day. So I go online, hoping to find someone who gets it—someone who can tell me how to keep the house running when I’m dizzy from anemia, or how to talk to my kids about why mommy is constantly crying or going to the doctor. I’m looking for a roadmap that accounts for my actual life, not the one they imagine I have.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the only thing I’d want to say is that I’m just so tired of being expected to hold it all together. People talk about the "joy" of pregnancy, or the "miracle" of it, but for someone like me—in my shoes, with my history—it feels like a long, slow trial. I’m not saying I don’t love this baby, because I do, with everything I have. But I feel like I’m being asked to pay for that love with every scrap of sanity I have left.

I wish that when a woman walks into that clinic, they wouldn't just see a "high-risk pregnancy." I wish they’d see the whole woman—the one who’s worried about her kids' clothes, the one whose jaw is aching, the one who’s terrified of being away from her family. I wish they’d stop giving me pamphlets about nutrition and start giving me a reason to believe that I’m actually going to be okay. 

I’m just an ordinary mom trying to do something extraordinary under circumstances that are breaking me. I hope, one day, maternity care looks less like a system of judgment and more like a system of support. Because right now, I feel like I’m surviving in spite of my care, not because of it. And that’s a heavy thing to carry into a delivery room.
